title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
text:
Getting your data onto the EBV Data Portal
The EBV Data Portal allows biodiversity datasets to be published, discovered, cited, and reused. By following the Essential Biodiversity Variables (EBVs) framework and the standardized EBVCube format, datasets from different projects can be compared and analyzed consistently.
Publishing an EBV dataset involves two steps. First, you create a valid EBVCube (a netCDF file) in R with the ebvcube package. Then, you upload the file through the EBV Data Portal, complete the metadata, submit it for review, and receive a DOI.
This tutorial guides you through the complete workflow, from raw raster to a published, citable dataset on the EBV Data Portal.
By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to:
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
text:
What is an EBV
Essential Biodiversity Variables (EBVs) are a set of standardized biological measurements that help scientists study, report and manage changes in biodiversity across time, space, and biological level. EBVs are group into six classes: genetic composition, species populations, species traits, community composition, ecosystem structure, and ecosystem function. EBV datasets are stored in the EBVCube format a CF and ACDD compliant netCDF file with four dimensions: longitude, latitude, time, and entity. This standardized format allows datasets from different sources to be interpreted and visualized consistently in the EBV Data Portal.
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
text:
The dataset we will use
This tutorial uses a single real, published dataset throughout.

Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM) — Martins, I., Pereira, H., Navarro, L. (2022).A community-composition EBV reporting the relative and absolute change in the number of bird species at decadal time steps from 1900 to 2010, for three entities: forest, non-forest, and all bird species.


Dataset page · DOI: 10.25829/8grx36 · EBV Portal id = 1 · netCDF ≈ 4 MB

We use this dataset (id = 1) throughout the tutorial: both when we explore the portal itself, its metadata form, entity list, DOI, citation, and versioning, and for the hands-on R code. Everything you see corresponds to something you can open yourself, right now, on the live portal.
To call the dataset, ebvcube fetches it directly from the portal using its numeric ID with ebv_download(id = 1, outputdir = "data/ebv", overwrite = FALSE) and caches it locally, so it only needs to be downloaded once. Every later code chunk starts from that file path; to run the workflow against your own data instead, just point it at your own .nc file and the rest of the tutorial runs unchanged.
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
text:
Workflow overview





flowchart LR
A["Prepare data<br/>rasters & metadata"]
B["Generate<br/>metadata JSON"]
C["ebv_create()<br/>create EBVCube"]
D["ebv_add_data()<br/>add data"]
E["Validate<br/>file"]
F["Upload<br/>to portal"]
G["Review & publish<br/>DOI"]

A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
text:
How this tutorial is organized



Unit
Topic




1
Introduction to EBVs and the EBV Data Portal


2
Installing R, RStudio, and the ebvcube package


3
Preparing your biodiversity data for conversion


4
Creating an EBVCube with ebvcube


5
Validating the generated file


6
Uploading your dataset to the EBV Data Portal


7
Troubleshooting common issues and best practices


8
Further resources, repositories, and references



Each unit opens with its learning objectives, works through the step with runnable code and screenshots, and closes with a short checkpoint so you know you are ready to move on.
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: From Raster to Portal: Creating and Publishing an EBVCube
text:
Key resources

EBV Data Portal — https://portal.geobon.org/ — discover, download, and publish EBV datasets.
ebvcube R package — https://github.com/EBVcube/ebvcube — read, visualize, and create EBVCubes. Also on CRAN and the b-cubed r-universe.
ebvcube documentation book — https://EBVcube.github.io/ebvcube/ — the full function reference this tutorial builds on.
EBVCube format specification — https://github.com/EBVcube/EBVCube-format — the structural rules behind the format.
GEO BON — What are EBVs? — https://geobon.org/ebvs/what-are-ebvs/ — the canonical definition and the six EBV classes.
Methods paper — Quoss et al. (2022), describing the package and the netCDF structure it implements.







Working alongside this tutorial. Every code chunk runs as-is against the bundled example dataset. If you are preparing your own dataset for the portal, follow the same steps but swap in your rasters and metadata where Units 3 and 4 point it out.



Ready? Start with Unit 1 — Introduction to EBVs and the EBV Data Portal.
crumbs: 1  Overview

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.1 What is an Essential Biodiversity Variable?
An Essential Biodiversity Variable (EBV) is a standardized, derived measurement that captures a key dimension of biodiversity and enables consistent monitoring of its status and change over time. The concept was proposed by Pereira et al. (2013) and is maintained by the Group on Earth Observations Biodiversity Observation Network (GEO BON). EBVs are produced by integrating and harmonizing multiple primary data sources, such as field observations, remote-sensing products, and model outputs, into a common, analysis-ready format. As a result, they provide an intermediate layer between raw biodiversity observations and the indicators used for biodiversity assessment, reporting, and decision making.
Think of an EBV as the biodiversity counterpart to an Essential Climate Variable: a small, agreed-upon set of variables that different research groups can produce, compare, and combine because they follow the same definitions. That shared standardization is what makes biodiversity data from different projects, taxa, and regions consistent enough to track over time.
crumbs: 2  Introduction

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.2 The six EBV classes
EBVs are organized into six classes, each capturing a different dimension of biodiversity:

Genetic composition
Species populations
Species traits
Community composition
Ecosystem structure
Ecosystem function


The dataset we use throughout this tutorial, Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM), belongs to the Community composition class.







Canonical classes vs. what you see on the portal



The six EBV classes above are the canonical classification defined by GEO BON. On the EBV Data Portal, you may not see all six listed at once, the portal shows only the classes for which datasets are currently available. You may also come across more specific labels such as Species distribution or Ecosystem disturbance; these are finer sub-categories within the canonical classes.

Ecosystem services is not one of the six EBV classes. It relates to a separate, complementary GEO BON framework, the Essential Ecosystem Service Variables (EESVs), which addresses the benefits people obtain from nature rather than biodiversity itself. For the official EBV classification, refer to the GEO BON definitions page.
crumbs: 2  Introduction

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.3 Why netCDF? The EBVCube format
A single EBV has a value at every location (longitude, latitude), at several points in time, and for each biological entity measured (a species, a group, an ecosystem type). Stored as separate map images, that becomes an unwieldy pile of files with no built-in context. The Network Common Data Form (netCDF), long used in the climate sciences, instead holds it all in one self-describing file: the data and the information that explains it units, dates, entities, projection and travel together.
The EBVCube is the standardized, netCDF based data format defined for EBVs. It follows two established conventions: CF v1.8 (how coordinates, units, and time are encoded) and ACDD v1.3 (the attributes portals use to index datasets), plus a few EBV specific rules set out in the EBVCube format specification.
Every data cube inside an EBVCube shares the same four dimensions, think of each pixel as carrying four pieces of information: where (longitude, latitude), when (time), and which entity:







Dimension
Meaning




lon
Longitude (or projected x-coordinate)


lat
Latitude (or projected y-coordinate)


time
Time steps (annual, decadal, scenario years, irregular)


entity
The biological unit (e.g. species, group of species, or ecosystem type)



The entity dimension allows a single EBVCube to contain data for multiple species or other biological entities. Beyond these four dimensions, metric and scenario groups organize different biodiversity measures or future projections within the same file. We return to that structure in Unit 4.






Figure 2.1: Overview of the EBVCube data model. An EBV dataset combines a hierarchical structure of scenarios and metrics with four-dimensional data cubes (longitude, latitude, time, and entity), and carries standardized metadata following the CF 1.8, ACDD 1.3, and EBV conventions. Source: Quoß et al. (in preparation).
crumbs: 2  Introduction

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.4 Touring the EBV Data Portal
All published EBV datasets are available through the EBV Data Portal. Throughout this tutorial, we will use a single dataset as our running example:






Worked example: Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM)




Reference: Martins, I., Pereira, H., Navarro, L. (2022). Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM) (Version 1) [Dataset]. German Centre for Integrative Biodiversity Research (iDiv) Halle-Jena-Leipzig. (https://doi.org/10.25829/8grx36)
EBV class: Community composition
ID: 1
DOI: 10.25829/8grx36



Open the dataset page and you will find everything that makes an EBV dataset FAIR (findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable):

a stable dataset ID (1) and a persistent DOI you can cite;
a structured JSON metadata record (title, abstract, EBV class, creators, license, entities);
the netCDF file itself, available for direct download (≈ 4 MB);
an interactive EBV Visualizer for exploring maps and temporal trends without downloading anything.

The three views you will use most are the Discover page (Figure 2.2), an individual dataset page (Figure 2.3), and the live EBV Visualizer below. Click either screenshot to enlarge it.

Discover pageDataset pageEBV Visualizer








Figure 2.2: The discover page showing the EBV class filters and a list of datasets.











Figure 2.3: The dataset page for id = 1, showing its gerneral metadata, and the netCDF download button.
















Portal interface as of July 2026. The portal is updated from time to time, so small layout differences are normal, the metadata, DOI, and download are always on the dataset page.



Browsing and inspecting datasets like this is how you find data on the portal. Next, we look at how you work with it.
crumbs: 2  Introduction

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.5 Where ebvcube fits
The portal is where EBV data is published and discovered; the ebvcube R package is how you work with it. It sits on both sides of the portal:

Reading published data. Give ebvcube a dataset’s numeric ID and it downloads the file, lists the data cubes inside, and reads, subsets, or maps them for you, pulling dataset id = 1 into R takes a couple of lines instead of a manual download and netCDF wrangling.
Producing new data. Starting from your own rasters or model output, ebvcube assembles them into a valid EBVCube, ready to upload and publish on the portal with its own DOI.

Read on one side, create on the other: that round trip is the workflow in the Overview diagram, and the path the rest of this tutorial follows, one step at a time.






Checkpoint. You just inspected dataset id = 1 on the portal, its EBV class (Community composition), its DOI, and its file format (netCDF). If those three make sense, you are ready to install the tools in Unit 2.
crumbs: 2  Introduction

title: 3  Installing software and the ebvcube package
text:
3.1 What you need first
Before installing ebvcube, make sure you have:
The lower-level libraries ebvcube relies on, HDF5 (for reading netCDF-4 files) and GDAL (for spatial data), are installed for you automatically on Windows and macOS: HDF5 comes with the Bioconductor package rhdf5, and GDAL comes with the CRAN build of terra. There is nothing extra to set up.
crumbs: 3  Installing software and the `ebvcube` package

title: 3  Installing software and the ebvcube package
text:
R ≥ 4.2.0: The minimum version supported by ebvcube. Check yours in any R session:

    R.version.string
If it is older, update R first, or CRAN will refuse to install the package.

RStudio (recommended): a free, widely used editor for R. It is not strictly required, but every code example in this tutorial assumes you are working in it.
crumbs: 3  Installing software and the `ebvcube` package

title: 3  Installing software and the ebvcube package
text:
3.2 Installing ebvcube from CRAN
For most users, the released version on CRAN is all you need:

install.packages("ebvcube")

CRAN resolves all of the CRAN dependencies automatically. Three dependencies, however, live on Bioconductor rather than CRAN, so on a fresh machine you will usually need the one-time step in the next section as well.






Other ways to install



Most people should use CRAN. If you need a newer feature, you can install the development version with devtools::install_github("EBVcube/ebvcube", ref = "dev"), or get the binary builds from the b-cubed r-universe. These are covered in the package’s installation guide.
crumbs: 3  Installing software and the `ebvcube` package

title: 3  Installing software and the ebvcube package
text:
3.3 Adding the Bioconductor dependencies
Three of ebvcube’s dependencies come from Bioconductor, because reading EBVCube files means reading HDF5 under the hood:







Package
Why it is needed




rhdf5
Low-level HDF5 read/write (netCDF-4 is HDF5 underneath)


DelayedArray
Lazy, on-disk arrays so large cubes do not exhaust your RAM


HDF5Array
Connects DelayedArray to HDF5 files



If installing ebvcube reports that one of these is missing, install them with BiocManager:

install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install(c("rhdf5", "DelayedArray", "HDF5Array"))

You only need to do this once per R installation.






Which Bioconductor version?



BiocManager automatically picks the Bioconductor release that matches your version of R, which is what you want. You can pin a specific release with BiocManager::install(version = "3.22"), but only do so if you have a reason to, each Bioconductor release requires a matching R version (3.22 needs R 4.5.x), so leaving it out is also a safe choice.
crumbs: 3  Installing software and the `ebvcube` package

title: 3  Installing software and the ebvcube package
text:
3.4 Verifying your installation
First, load the package and confirm the version:

library(ebvcube)
packageVersion("ebvcube")

Then open the small example dataset that ships with the package, a subset of the Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM) dataset. Because it is bundled, this needs no download and works offline:

file <- system.file("extdata", "martins_comcom_subset.nc", package = "ebvcube")

prop <- ebv_properties(file, verbose = FALSE)
prop@general$title
#> [1] "Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM)"

If the title prints, your installation is working. When you are ready to pull the full dataset from the portal (≈ 4 MB), the same two lines work with a download instead of the bundled file. We do exactly that in the next unit:

file <- ebv_download(id = 1, outputdir = tempdir(), overwrite = FALSE)
prop <- ebv_properties(file, verbose = FALSE)
prop@general$title







Checkpoint. library(ebvcube) loads with no error, and ebv_properties() prints the dataset title. If both work, your setup is complete and you are ready to prepare biodiversity data in Unit 3.
crumbs: 3  Installing software and the `ebvcube` package

title: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata
text:
4.1 What ebv_create() needs
ebv_create() does not read your rasters and guess what belongs in the file. It reads a metadata file you prepare first, builds an empty netCDF with exactly the structure that metadata describes, then leaves it for you to fill. Almost all the work happens before you call the function.
Three things have to be ready:
The metadata defines the structure of the EBVCube, while the data layers provide the values stored within that structure. The entity list provides the link between biological units and their corresponding layers.
crumbs: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata

title: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata
text:
Input
What it is
Where it comes from




A metadata JSON
The file’s identity and structure as text: title, EBV class, license, spatial and temporal coverage, and one entry per metric.
Fill in the metadata form on the EBV Data Portal and download it (recommended), or build the same file in R with ebv_metadata().


Your data
One 2-D layer (lon × lat) for every entity, time step, and metric, usually a stack of GeoTIFFs or in-memory rasters.
The output of your modelling or analysis.


An entity list
The names of the biological units the file describes, in the exact order the layers are stacked.
You decide it. For dataset id = 1 it is three entities: forest, non-forest, and all bird species.










Metadata first, structure second



Whether you generate the metadata JSON from the EBV Data Portal or with ebv_metadata(), review it before running ebv_create(). If you are using the portal, you can return to the form at any time, update the metadata, and download a new JSON file. Once the metadata accurately represents your dataset, use this final JSON file to build the EBVCube.
crumbs: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata

title: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata
text:
4.2 Dimensions, metrics, and scenarios
An EBVCube organizes biodiversity data along four core dimensions: lon, lat, time, and entity. As the data producer, you define each of them when creating your cube.
lon and lat set the spatial grid. All layers must share the same extent, resolution, and CRS, which are provided to ebv_create() through the arguments extent, resolution, and epsg.
time defines the temporal dimension of your dataset, from a single observation date to a time series. The temporal range and interval are specified in the metadata and must match the layers added to the cube.
entity represents the biological unit described by each layer, such as a species, a group of species, or an ecosystem type. In the example dataset (id=1), the entities are forest, non-forest, and all bird species. The order of the entity list must match the order of your layers, because this is how the cube knows which biological unit each layer represents.
Metric and scenario are not dimensions but define how the cube is organized within the netCDF file. A metric describes the variable being measured or calculated (e.g., relative change in species richness), and every dataset contains at least one metric. A scenario is optional and groups the same metric under different projected pathways (e.g., SSP1 or SSP5).
For datasets without scenarios, the cube is stored as: metric_1/ebv_cube
With scenarios, the structure becomes: scenario_1/metric_1/ebv_cube
crumbs: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata

title: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata
text:
4.3 Pre-flight checklist
Before building the EBVCube, verify that your data and metadata describe the same structure. These checks can be performed while preparing the metadata, either through the portal form or with ebv_metadata(). Since the metadata defines the EBVCube structure, resolving inconsistencies at this stage avoids problems during data insertion with ebv_add_data().

All layers share one grid. Same extent, resolution, and CRS across every GeoTIFF. Check with terra::rast() and compare.
Layer count is deliberate. The number of layers equals entities × time steps × metrics (× scenarios, if you have them). If that product does not match your stack, something is missing or duplicated.
Entity names are final and ordered. One name per entity, in the same order as your layers.
A fill value is chosen. Decide what marks “no data” (the format default is -3.4e+38) and confirm your rasters actually use it for gaps.
You know your coverage exactly. Start year, end year, temporal spacing, bounding box, EPSG code. These are the values you enter into the metadata form, or pass to ebv_metadata().
File format is consistent. Confirm that all input layers can be read correctly by terra and have the expected data type and missing-value representation







Confirm the grid in one step




library(terra)
layers <- rast(list.files("data/tifs", pattern = "\\.tif$",
                          full.names = TRUE))
layers            # inspect extent, resolution, CRS, and nlyr()

If every layer prints the same extent and resolution, and nlyr() matches your expected count, the grid is consistent.
crumbs: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata

title: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata
text:
4.4 Getting the metadata JSON
Every EBVCube starts from a metadata JSON, and there are two ways to produce one. Which you pick depends on whether you are publishing through the portal or scripting a pipeline.

4.4.1 Path A — the portal metadata form (recommended)
The simplest way to create a valid metadata JSON is by using the Uploads → New Upload form in the EBV Data Portal. The form is organized into three tabs. Complete the required fields in the first two tabs, then use the third tab to generate and download the metadata JSON file.
Each tab contains more fields than fit on a single screen, so the images below show only the top portion of each tab. Be sure to scroll through the entire form and complete all fields marked with a red asterisk (*).

General informationEBV attributesData upload


Title, creation date, a summary, methods, and any related DOIs, among other general fields.






Figure 4.1: New upload — General information.





The controlled vocabulary fields: EBV class, EBV name, and entity type, chosen from fixed lists so they can’t be mistyped. Below them you add one or more metrics (each with a name, description, and units) and, if your data has them, one or more scenarios, among other EBV attribute fields further down.






Figure 4.2: New upload — EBV attributes.





The portal generates the metadata JSON here. Click Download Metadata to save the .json; that file is the input to ebv_create(). This same tab is where you return later to upload the finished netCDF (option 2, “using the R package ebvcube”).






Figure 4.3: New upload — Data upload, with the Download Metadata link.












You need a GEO BON account



The form sits behind a login. Create a free account at portal.geobon.org before you start.




4.4.2 Path B — from R with ebv_metadata()
You can also build the same JSON entirely in R. This fits an automated or reproducible pipeline, where generating the metadata from a script beats filling a form by hand. ebv_metadata() takes one argument per field the form would ask for, writes the .json to disk, and returns its path. The controlled-vocabulary fields still have to match the portal’s accepted terms, so ?ebv_metadata stays the authoritative reference for what each one takes.

json <- ebv_metadata(
  outputpath               = "data/my_ebv_metadata.json",
  title                    = "Local bird diversity (worked example)",
  creator_name             = "Jane Doe",
  creator_institution      = "Example University",
  coverage_content_type    = "Model result",
  ebv_class                = "Community composition",
  ebv_name                 = "Taxonomic and phylogenetic diversity",
  ebv_spatial_scope        = "Global",
  ebv_domain               = "Terrestrial",
  ebv_entity_type          = "Communities",
  ebv_entity_scope         = "Bird communities",
  time_coverage_start      = "1900-01-01",
  time_coverage_end        = "2010-01-01",
  metric = list(
    standard_name = "Relative change in the number of species",
    long_name     = "Relative change in the number of bird species relative to 1900",
    units         = "percent"
  ),
  overwrite = TRUE,
  verbose   = FALSE
)

A few fields to watch:

ebv_class must match one of the classes the portal lists (the canonical six plus Ecosystem services and Cross-cutting), spelled exactly as shown.
coverage_content_type records how the data were produced: modelResult for modelled data, image for direct observation, and a handful of other CF values.
metric is a list. For several metrics, pass a list of lists and the file gains metric_2, metric_3, and so on. To add scenarios, pass a scenario argument the same way; leave it out, as here, and the file stays flat.

Both paths end in the same place: a .json on disk that describes the file you are about to build. Whichever you use, open it in a text editor and read it once before moving on. Editing a line by hand is fine, and often quicker than regenerating the whole file for a one-word fix.






Match the JSON to your data, not the other way around



The metadata declares a shape: so many metrics, so many time steps, this grid. ebv_create() builds exactly that shape, and ebv_add_data() refuses anything that does not fit. If the two disagree, correct whichever one is wrong now, while it is still a line in a JSON file.
crumbs: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata

title: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata
section: 4.5 Downloading dataset 1 for a working reference
text:
4.5 Downloading dataset 1 for a working reference
The example dataset from Unit 1 is used here because it represents a complete EBVCube and allows you to compare your own metadata and file structure against an existing example: the Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM) dataset (id = 1)

library(ebvcube)

dir.create("data/ebv", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
file <- ebv_download(id = 1, outputdir = "data/ebv",
                     overwrite = FALSE)

ebv_datacubepaths(file, verbose = FALSE)

The paths come back as metric_*/ebv_cube with no scenario segment, which confirms what Section 4.2 covered. This is a file without a scenario layer. Read its metadata the same way you did as a consumer, and treat each field as a slot you will need to fill for your own dataset:

prop <- ebv_properties(file, verbose = FALSE)
prop@general$title
prop@general$ebv_class
prop@spatial$srs
prop@temporal$resolution

Whatever this file declares, yours will declare too. The difference is that here the values are already filled in, and for your dataset you are the one supplying them.






Checkpoint. Your data share one grid, and the number of layers matches entities × time steps × metrics (× scenarios, if any). Your entity list is final and ordered, and you have a metadata JSON on disk, from the portal form or ebv_metadata(), that you have read through. If that all holds, and your metric and scenario structure matches your dataset, you are ready to build the netCDF in Chapter 5.
crumbs: 4  Preparing biodiversity data and metadata

title: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the ebvcube package
text:
5.1 Writing the empty netCDF with ebv_create()
ebv_create() reads the metadata JSON and writes a netCDF that has the right shape but no data yet. You supply the entity names here, along with the grid the cubes sit on: the projection (epsg), the geographic extent, and the resolution in degrees. The running example has three entities and a 1° global grid.
entities <- c("forest bird species",
              "non-forest bird species",
              "all bird species")

ebv_create(
  jsonpath   = json,
  outputpath = new_nc,
  entities   = entities,
  epsg       = 4326,
  extent     = c(-180, 180, -90, 90),
  resolution = c(1, 1),
  fillvalue  = -3.4e+38,
  overwrite  = TRUE,
  verbose    = FALSE
)
At this point the file is fully structured but every cell still holds the fill value. Check the skeleton before you add anything to it:
ebv_properties(new_nc, verbose = FALSE)@general$entity_names

ebv_datacubepaths(new_nc, verbose = FALSE)
The entity names should match what you passed in, and the datacube paths tell you where the data will live. For the running example there is no scenario layer, so the cubes sit at metric_1/ebv_cube.
crumbs: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the `ebvcube` package

title: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the ebvcube package
text:
5.2 Adding your data with ebv_add_data()
Each entity’s data lives in its own GeoTIFF (or in-memory raster), and you add them one at a time. In a real workflow these files are the output of your analysis. Here we synthesize three rasters on the same 1° grid as the netCDF, so the example runs without any bundled files.

# Three 3-band rasters, one per entity, on the netCDF's grid.
template <- terra::rast(terra::ext(-180, 180, -90, 90),
                        resolution = 1, crs = "EPSG:4326")
tifs <- file.path(tempdir(), c("entity1.tif", "entity2.tif", "entity3.tif"))
for (i in seq_along(tifs)) {
  r3 <- terra::rast(template, nlyrs = 3)
  terra::values(r3) <- matrix(runif(terra::ncell(r3) * 3), ncol = 3)
  terra::writeRaster(r3, tifs[i], overwrite = TRUE)
}

for (i in seq_along(tifs)) {
  ebv_add_data(
    filepath_nc = new_nc,
    metric      = 1,       # add to metric_1
    entity      = i,
    timestep    = 1:3,     # first three time steps
    data        = tifs[i],
    band        = 1:3,     # bands 1-3 of the tif
    verbose     = FALSE
  )
}

You can call ebv_add_data() again on the same cube to fill in more time steps later. It appends rather than overwrites, as long as each call keeps the (metric, entity, timestep) combination unique.
crumbs: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the `ebvcube` package

title: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the ebvcube package
text:
5.3 Correcting an attribute with ebv_attribute()
If you later spot a wrong unit, a typo in a description, or any other attribute that needs fixing, you can edit it in place without rebuilding the file:

ebv_attribute(new_nc,
              attribute_name = "units",
              value          = "Percentage",
              levelpath      = "metric_1/ebv_cube")

ebv_properties(new_nc, "metric_1/ebv_cube",
               verbose = FALSE)@ebv_cube$units
#> [1] "Percentage"

The levelpath argument points at the layer you want to change. Pass "metric_1/ebv_cube" for the cube itself, "metric_1" for the metric, or "/" for a global attribute.
crumbs: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the `ebvcube` package

title: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the ebvcube package
text:
5.4 Species-level datasets with ebv_create_taxonomy()
When your entities are individual species rather than aggregated groups, use ebv_create_taxonomy() in place of ebv_create(). Instead of a plain vector of entity names, you supply a table with one row per species and one column per taxonomic rank. The function writes an extra taxonomy variable into the netCDF that the EBV Data Portal and downstream tools can use to filter and group species.

# The column names you choose become the taxonomic ranks stored in the file.
# In practice this table usually comes from a GBIF or Catalogue of Life export.
taxonomy <- data.frame(
  species = c("Hirundo rustica", "Apus apus",   "Falco peregrinus"),
  genus   = c("Hirundo",         "Apus",        "Falco"),
  family  = c("Hirundinidae",    "Apodidae",    "Falconidae"),
  order   = c("Passeriformes",   "Apodiformes", "Falconiformes"),
  class   = c("Aves",            "Aves",        "Aves")
)
taxonomy_csv <- file.path(tempdir(), "taxonomy.csv")
write.csv(taxonomy, taxonomy_csv, row.names = FALSE)

new_nc_tax <- file.path(tempdir(), "bird_diversity_taxonomy.nc")

ebv_create_taxonomy(
  jsonpath   = json,
  outputpath = new_nc_tax,
  taxonomy   = taxonomy_csv,
  sep        = ",",
  epsg       = 4326,
  extent     = c(-180, 180, -90, 90),
  resolution = c(1, 1),
  fillvalue  = -3.4e+38,
  overwrite  = TRUE,
  verbose    = FALSE
)

From here ebv_add_data() works exactly as it did above. The taxonomy is read-only metadata and does not change how you fill the cubes. See ?ebv_create_taxonomy for the accepted column names and separator options.
crumbs: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the `ebvcube` package

title: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the ebvcube package
text:
5.5 Checking the finished file
Once the cubes are filled, confirm the file one more time before you validate it. ebv_properties() reports the general metadata and structure, and ebv_datacubepaths() lists every cube in the file:

ebv_properties(new_nc, verbose = FALSE)

ebv_datacubepaths(new_nc, verbose = FALSE)







A visual check with Panoply



Panoply is a free NASA viewer for netCDF and HDF5 files. Drag your new .nc into it and it shows the group hierarchy, dimensions, and attributes side by side, and it can draw a quick map of any cube. It is a fast way to see whether the file looks the way you expect while you are still learning the format.








Checkpoint. You have written an empty netCDF from your metadata, filled its cubes with ebv_add_data(), and confirmed the structure with ebv_properties() and ebv_datacubepaths(). If the datacube paths list the entities you expect and the values read back, your EBVCube is ready to validate in Chapter 6.
crumbs: 5  Creating an EBVCube with the `ebvcube` package

title: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading
text:
6.1 Check the structure
Start with the two functions you already met. ebv_datacubepaths() lists every cube in the file, and ebv_properties() reports the metadata slot by slot. Read them against what you meant to build:
ebv_datacubepaths(nc, verbose = FALSE)

props <- ebv_properties(nc, verbose = FALSE)
props@general$entity_names     # the entities you declared
props@spatial$extent           # c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
props@temporal$dates           # the time axis
The datacube paths should list every metric and entity you expect, the entity names should read correctly, and the extent and dates should match your data. A missing entity or an empty time axis points to a step that went wrong in creation.
crumbs: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading

title: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading
text:
6.2 Check the values
Structure can be right while the numbers are wrong, so read a slice and look at its range. ebv_analyse() returns summary statistics for a cube or a subset:

stats <- ebv_analyse(nc, dc, entity = 1, timestep = 1, verbose = FALSE)
stats[c("min", "max", "mean", "n", "NAs")]

n is the number of valid pixels. If it is zero, or NAs covers the whole grid, the cube never received its data. A min or max far outside what your variable should hold usually means a unit problem or a stray fill value that leaked into the data.
crumbs: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading

title: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading
text:
6.3 Check it visually
Numbers only tell you so much. ebv_map() draws a single slice, and one look often catches problems a summary hides, such as data in the wrong hemisphere or an all-empty ocean:

ebv_map(nc, dc, entity = 1, timestep = 1, verbose = FALSE)







If the map looks shifted by 180°



The file is using the [0, 360] longitude convention while the basemap expects [-180, 180]. Read the slice and wrap it before plotting:
r <- ebv_read(nc, dc, entity = 1, timestep = 1, type = "r")
r <- terra::rotate(r)
crumbs: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading

title: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading
text:
6.4 Compare against your metadata
Finally, confirm the file says what you told it to say. Read the attributes back and check them against the metadata form (or the ebv_metadata() call) you used to create it:

props@general$title
ebv_properties(nc, dc, verbose = FALSE)@ebv_cube$units

The title, units, entity count, and time coverage should all match your metadata. If a unit or description is wrong, fix it in place with ebv_attribute() from Chapter 5 rather than rebuilding the file.
crumbs: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading

title: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading
text:
6.5 A pre-flight checklist
Before you move to the portal, walk through this list. If every box ticks, the file is ready to upload:

The datacube paths list every metric and entity you expect.
Entity names, dates, extent, and resolution match your metadata.
Units and the cube description read correctly.
A map of at least one slice looks right, with no 180° shift.
Value ranges are plausible and the valid-pixel count (n) is not zero.

The portal runs its own validation when you submit, but clearing these first saves a round trip. Panoply, introduced in Chapter 5, is a quick way to eyeball the structure outside R if you want a second opinion.






Checkpoint. Your file’s structure, values, and map all match the dataset you meant to create, and every box in the pre-flight checklist is ticked. If so, you are ready to upload it to the EBV Data Portal in Chapter 7.
crumbs: 6  Validating your EBVCube before uploading

title: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal
text:
7.1 Back to the upload form
The submission uses the same New Upload form you met in Chapter 4. Sign in with your GEO BON account and reopen your draft under Uploads. The General information and EBV attributes tabs are already filled from earlier, so the only tab left to finish is Data upload.
crumbs: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal

title: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal
text:
7.2 Uploading your netCDF
On the Data upload tab the portal offers two ways to submit: a ZIP of GeoTIFFs, or a netCDF built with the ebvcube package. You built the netCDF, so choose that option and upload your .nc file.
If the form asks for a preview image, add one. A small map of a single slice, exported from ebv_map(), is enough and makes your dataset easier to recognize in the catalogue. The portal runs its own validation when you upload; if it flags anything, work back through the checks in Chapter 6, fix the file, and upload it again.
crumbs: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal

title: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal
text:
7.3 Submitting for review
Once the file is in, submit the dataset for review. It moves out of Draft into a review state, where the portal team checks the metadata and the file against the EBV standard before it goes public. Expect a wait, and possibly a request for small changes. When it is approved, the dataset is published: it appears in the public catalogue and becomes downloadable with ebv_download().
crumbs: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal

title: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal
section: 7.4 After publication: DOI, citation, and versions
text:
7.4 After publication: DOI, citation, and versions
On publication the portal assigns your dataset a DOI and generates its citation, both shown on the dataset page. You can see this on the live page for the running example, dataset id = 1, where its version history, DOI, and citation all appear. Point people to those when they cite your data, not to the ebvcube package citation, which credits the software rather than the dataset.
If you later revise the data, upload it as a new version instead of editing the published one. The portal keeps versions distinct, so anyone who already cited the earlier DOI still lands on the exact data they used.
Here is that page, embedded live (in the HTML version of the book):



If the page does not load, open dataset id = 1 on the EBV Data Portal.







Checkpoint. Your netCDF is uploaded, submitted, and, once approved, live on the EBV Data Portal with its own DOI. If anything snagged along the way, Chapter 8 collects the fixes for the problems that come up most often.
crumbs: 7  Uploading the dataset to the EBV Data Portal

title: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help
text:
8.1 Installation
These are the problems that stop library(ebvcube) from loading. The full setup is in Chapter 3.
crumbs: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help

title: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help
text:
8.1.1 The Bioconductor packages are not found
there is no package called 'rhdf5' (or DelayedArray, or HDF5Array). These live on Bioconductor, not CRAN, so install.packages("ebvcube") cannot pull them in on its own:

install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install(c("rhdf5", "DelayedArray", "HDF5Array"))

Then retry library(ebvcube).


8.1.2 package 'ebvcube' is not available for this version of R
You are on an older R, and Bioconductor releases are tied to specific R versions. Either update R to the current release, which is the cleaner fix, or install an older ebvcube that supports your R version:

devtools::install_github("EBVcube/ebvcube@v0.5.0")   # adjust the tag



8.1.3 Compilation fails on Linux with HDF5 or GDAL errors
You are missing the system libraries the R packages compile against. On Debian or Ubuntu:
sudo apt-get install libhdf5-dev libgdal-dev libproj-dev libgeos-dev libudunits2-dev
Then reinstall the dependency that failed (rhdf5, terra, and so on), not ebvcube itself, since the failure is in the dependency.


8.1.4 HDF5 library version mismatched
Your installed rhdf5 was built against a different HDF5 library than the one loaded at runtime. Reinstall it from a clean R session:

remove.packages("rhdf5")
BiocManager::install("rhdf5", force = TRUE)
crumbs: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help

title: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help
text:
8.2 Preparing data and metadata
Most metadata trouble comes from the controlled vocabularies. See Chapter 4 for the full form.

8.2.1 A metadata value is rejected
Fields such as ebv_class, ebv_name, and ebv_entity_type accept only the terms the portal knows. If ebv_metadata() or the upload form refuses a value, it almost always does not match one of those terms. Copy the wording from the portal form’s pick lists, or check ?ebv_metadata for the accepted list, rather than typing your own phrasing.
crumbs: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help

title: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help
text:
8.3 Creating the cube
Errors here usually mean the metadata and the data disagree about shape. The build itself is in Chapter 5.

8.3.1 entities length does not match metadata
The entities vector you passed to ebv_create() has a different length than the entity count declared in the JSON. Fix one so the two agree.


8.3.2 ebv_add_data() fails with a dimension mismatch
The GeoTIFF you are adding does not sit on the same grid as the netCDF, so its resolution, extent, or CRS is off. Reproject it onto the netCDF’s grid before adding it:

target_r <- ebv_read(new_nc, "metric_1/ebv_cube",
                     entity = 1, timestep = 1, type = "r")

r  <- terra::rast("your_data.tif")
r2 <- terra::project(r, target_r)          # match CRS, resolution, extent
terra::writeRaster(r2, "your_data_aligned.tif", overwrite = TRUE)

Add your_data_aligned.tif instead of the original.


8.3.3 Units, titles, or descriptions look wrong after ebv_create()
You do not need to rebuild the file. Fix the attribute in place with ebv_attribute(), as shown in Chapter 5.
crumbs: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help

title: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help
text:
8.4 Reading the file back
These come up when you validate your file or read a published one. The checks are in Chapter 6.

8.4.1 ebv_read() says “Not enough RAM”
The cube you asked for is larger than the package thinks your machine can hold. Two ways out: subset it spatially first with ebv_read_bb() or ebv_read_shp(), or read it lazily with type = "da" to get a DelayedArray. Note that ignore_RAM = TRUE only switches off the safety check; it does not make the read lazy.


8.4.2 ebv_read_shp() returns an array full of NA
Two usual causes. Either the shapefile is in a different projection from the netCDF, so reproject it to match:

shp_p <- sf::st_transform(sf::st_read(shp), 4326)
sf::st_write(shp_p, "shp_wgs84.shp")

Or the shapefile and the file’s extent do not overlap at all. Compare them before reading:

ebv_properties(nc, verbose = FALSE)@spatial$extent   # c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
sf::st_bbox(sf::st_read(shp))



8.4.3 The map looks shifted by 180°
The file uses the [0, 360] longitude convention while your basemap expects [-180, 180]. Read the slice and wrap it with terra::rotate(), as described in Chapter 6.
crumbs: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help

title: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help
text:
8.5 Uploading to the portal
The submission steps are in Chapter 7; here are the snags that interrupt them.

8.5.1 The upload is rejected
The portal validates your file when you upload it. If it pushes the file back, the fault is almost always something the pre-flight checks in Chapter 6 would have caught, so re-run those, fix the file, and upload again. A very large file can also be slow or time out; before you assume the portal is at fault, make sure the file is not larger than it needs to be.


8.5.2 A value you need is not in a pick list
The form’s controlled vocabularies are deliberate. Choose the closest listed term rather than forcing your own, and if nothing fits, ask the portal team through the contact route on the dataset page rather than working around it.


8.5.3 The dataset is stuck in review
Review is done by people, so it takes time, and a request for small changes is normal. If a long stretch passes with no word, follow up through the dataset page contact or GEO BON.
crumbs: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help

title: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help
text:
8.6 Getting help and reporting bugs
If none of the above fixes it, a good bug report is the fastest way forward. Package bugs go to the GitHub issue tracker, and a report the maintainers can act on includes:

the output of sessionInfo(),
a minimal reproducible example, ideally on a dataset that ships with the package,
the full error message, and
what you expected to happen.

For open questions rather than bugs, use the GitHub Discussions board. Before posting either, check you are on the latest release with packageVersion("ebvcube"), since the fix may already be out.
Two more things worth knowing. To cite the package, run citation("ebvcube"), which prints both a plain-text and a BibTeX entry; to cite a dataset, use its own DOI from the portal page rather than the package citation (see Chapter 7). And the package is open source under GPL-3, with the source on GitHub, so pull requests are welcome.






Checkpoint. You can find the stage where you got stuck, apply the fix, and, if it turns out to be a genuine bug, file a report the maintainers can act on. That closes the whole arc, from a raster on your disk to a published dataset with a DOI. For further reading and references, see Chapter 9.
crumbs: 8  Troubleshooting and getting help

title: 9  Further reading and references
text:
9.1 Documentation and the format specification
For installing the package, see Chapter 3; for where to ask questions or report a bug, see Chapter 8.
crumbs: 9  Further reading and references

title: 9  Further reading and references
text:
EBV netCDF format specification — the authoritative description of the EBVCube format: https://github.com/EBVcube/EBVCube-format
ebvcube documentation book — the full package reference, including advanced usage beyond this tutorial: https://EBVcube.github.io/ebvcube/
ebvcube source on GitHub — code and release history: https://github.com/EBVcube/ebvcube
Running example dataset — “Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM)”, the dataset followed throughout this book: https://portal.geobon.org/ebv-detail?id=1 (DOI 10.25829/8grx36). Its official citation is on the dataset page.
crumbs: 9  Further reading and references

title: 9  Further reading and references
text: 9.2 References
crumbs: 9  Further reading and references
